metadata.constraint: Agent limited to official pytorch.org documentation only. GitHub issues, forums, blogs excluded.
metadata.notes: Held-out validation set — 24 cases not used for rubric development

results.agent_guidance:
The official PyTorch documentation on FakeTensor and PrivateUse1 backends provides relevant context for this issue, though it does not specifically address the interaction between FakeTensor.avoid_device_init and privateuse1 device placement.

The Fake Tensor documentation (https://docs.pytorch.org/docs/stable/user_guide/torch_compiler/torch.compiler_fake_tensor.html) explains that FakeTensors are tensors with metadata but no real data, used during torch.compile tracing to infer output shapes and dtypes without executing real operations. The page describes common issues such as data-dependent operations and tensor metadata mismatches, but does not explicitly discuss how FakeTensor interacts with custom device backends or how avoid_device_init handles privateuse1 device types.

The PrivateUse1 tutorial (https://docs.pytorch.org/tutorials/advanced/privateuseone.html) provides a comprehensive guide for integrating custom backends via the PrivateUse1 mechanism. It covers registering a custom device module with torch, renaming privateuse1 to your custom device name, generating device-specific methods, and registering custom device-specific implementations of operators. However, the tutorial does not cover how embedded constants are placed during compilation or how FakeTensor's device initialization interacts with custom backends.

Since this issue was resolved and the official pytorch.org documentation does not directly address the FakeTensor.avoid_device_init behavior with privateuse1 backends, the fix was likely an internal code change to ensure that the device placement logic in FakeTensor correctly respects the registered privateuse1 backend. If you are developing a custom backend via privateuse1, ensure you have fully registered your backend following the tutorial, including setting up proper device guards and dispatch keys.

The official PyTorch documentation on CUDA semantics and torch.compile provides partial context for this issue, but does not specifically document how CUDA stream operations are traced by the compiler.

The CUDA semantics documentation (https://docs.pytorch.org/docs/main/notes/cuda.html) explains PyTorch's stream model: each device has a default stream, operations are serialized on the same stream, and torch.cuda.Stream can be used to run operations concurrently. The torch.cuda API reference documents torch.cuda.current_stream() and the Stream class, including attributes like cuda_stream. However, neither page discusses how these stream operations behave under torch.compile.

The torch.compile troubleshooting guide (https://docs.pytorch.org/docs/2.7/torch.compiler_troubleshooting.html) discusses common issues like graph breaks, recompilation, and accuracy errors, but does not specifically address CUDA stream tracing. Stream operations like torch.cuda.current_stream().cuda_stream involve querying runtime state that may not be accurately captured during Dynamo's symbolic tracing.

Since this issue is marked as resolved, it was likely fixed as a compiler bug. If you encounter similar issues, consider wrapping stream-dependent code in torch.compiler.disable() to prevent it from being traced.

The official PyTorch documentation provides limited coverage of AArch64-specific issues with torch.compile's Inductor CPU backend and int8 weight-only quantized operations.

The Inductor CPU backend debugging and profiling tutorial (https://docs.pytorch.org/tutorials/intermediate/inductor_debug_cpu.html) explains how to debug and profile torch.compile on CPU, including using TORCH_COMPILE_DEBUG to inspect generated code. This tutorial is useful for diagnosing test failures on CPU but focuses primarily on x86 architectures and does not discuss AArch64-specific codegen paths.

The PyTorch quantization documentation (https://docs.pytorch.org/docs/stable/quantization.html) covers quantization workflows but does not specifically address int8 weight-only quantized matmul kernel support on AArch64 platforms.

This issue is unresolved. The test failures for test_int8_woq_mm_* on Neoverse-N1 likely indicate that the select-algorithm path for int8 weight-only quantized matmul does not have a correct or optimized kernel implementation for this ARM microarchitecture. The official documentation does not provide a workaround. This appears to be a platform support gap in the Inductor CPU backend's codegen for AArch64.

The official PyTorch documentation on scaled_dot_product_attention (SDPA) and torch.compile with dynamic shapes provides relevant context.

The torch.nn.functional.scaled_dot_product_attention API reference (https://docs.pytorch.org/docs/stable/generated/torch.nn.functional.scaled_dot_product_attention.html) documents the SDPA function, which automatically selects between FlashAttention, Memory-Efficient Attention, and a C++ math fallback. Different backend implementations have different constraints on input shapes and dtypes, which can cause failures when dynamic shapes introduce sizes a particular backend does not support.

The torch.compile troubleshooting documentation covers common issues with compilation, including accuracy problems and graph breaks. Dynamic shapes can interact poorly with operations that have shape-dependent kernel selection, like SDPA.

Since this is a HuggingFace transformers test failing with a torch nightly build, the issue likely stems from a compatibility gap between the transformers library's SDPA usage patterns and a recent change in the nightly's compilation or SDPA dispatch logic. If you encounter similar failures, try setting torch.backends.cuda.enable_flash_sdp(False) or torch.backends.cuda.enable_mem_efficient_sdp(False) to isolate which SDPA backend is causing the failure. Since this issue was resolved, it was likely fixed in a subsequent nightly build.

The official PyTorch documentation on custom backends for torch.compile is directly relevant. The Custom Backends documentation (https://docs.pytorch.org/docs/2.6/torch.compiler_custom_backends.html) explains how to register a custom backend and how the backend receives an FX graph and example inputs, then returns a compiled callable.

The issue -- fusion failing after make_boxed_func -- points to a problem with operation ordering. When you capture the graph via torch.dynamo and then apply torch._inductor.pattern_matcher for operator fusion, the fusion passes must complete before make_boxed_func wraps the graph. make_boxed_func changes how inputs are handled (converting to the boxed calling convention expected by AOTAutograd), and any pattern matching after this wrapping would fail because the graph structure no longer matches expected patterns.

The correct order is: (1) capture graph via Dynamo, (2) apply AOTAutograd if needed, (3) run custom fusion passes via pattern_matcher, (4) apply make_boxed_func. Reversing steps 3 and 4 will cause fusion to silently fail. The official documentation does not explicitly warn about this ordering constraint, which represents a documentation gap for users building custom backends that combine pattern_matcher with AOTAutograd internals.

The official PyTorch documentation provides some context on attention optimization but does not specifically document the merge_attention operation or its performance characteristics with transposed inputs on Blackwell GPUs.

The FlexAttention blog post (https://pytorch.org/blog/flexattention/) introduces FlexAttention as a flexible, high-performance attention API. The Helion blog post (https://pytorch.org/blog/helion/) introduces Helion as a DSL for writing performant GPU kernels. If the Helion kernel significantly outperforms torch.compile's generated code, particularly on Blackwell GPUs with transposed inputs, this suggests that Inductor's code generation is not effectively handling the memory access patterns of transposed inputs.

Blackwell (SM_120) is a very new architecture, and Inductor's heuristics may not yet be optimized for it. The Performance Tuning Guide provides general GPU performance tips but does not address architecture-specific tuning. Since this issue is unresolved, the current recommendation would be to either use the Helion kernel directly as a custom operator, or investigate whether the transposed inputs can be made contiguous before the merge_attention call.

The official PyTorch documentation on torch.autocast (https://pytorch.org/docs/stable/amp.html) describes how autocast context managers manage dtype casting. The torch.compile troubleshooting guide (https://pytorch.org/docs/stable/torch.compiler_troubleshooting.html) describes how to diagnose correctness issues using the minifier and by setting torch._dynamo.config.repro_after.

The troubleshooting documentation recommends a systematic debugging approach: first confirm the issue by comparing eager vs. compiled outputs, then use the TORCHDYNAMO_REPRO_AFTER environment variable to isolate the problematic subgraph. For autocast specifically, the AMP documentation notes that autocast should be applied as a context manager around the forward pass. Dynamic attribute access via getattr can be problematic because Dynamo must resolve attribute lookups at trace time; attributes that change dynamically may cause guard failures or correctness issues.

The official docs do not provide a specific section on the intersection of autocast contexts with dynamic attribute access under torch.compile. The recommended approach is to use torch._dynamo.explain() to inspect what Dynamo is doing, check for graph breaks around the autocast region, and verify that dynamic attributes are not being incorrectly specialized during tracing.

The PyTorch documentation on channels_last memory format (https://pytorch.org/tutorials/intermediate/memory_format_tutorial.html) explains that channels_last (NHWC) is an alternative memory layout and warns that not all operators fully support it. The function torch.as_strided creates a view with specified size, stride, and offset; the documentation (https://pytorch.org/docs/stable/generated/torch.Tensor.as_strided.html) warns it is an advanced and unsafe operation.

When a tensor is in channels_last format, its strides differ from contiguous layout, so hardcoded stride values that assume contiguous (NCHW) layout will produce incorrect views. The torch.compile troubleshooting guide describes how the Inductor backend may apply its own memory layout optimizations, which can further interact with channels_last tensors in ways that differ from eager mode.

The official documentation does not specifically document the as_strided + channels_last + Inductor interaction as a known bug, but the general guidance is to ensure that any manual stride manipulation accounts for the actual memory format of the input tensor. If you see correctness divergence, convert the tensor to contiguous format before applying as_strided, or use higher-level operations that handle memory format automatically.

The official PyTorch documentation for torch.full (https://pytorch.org/docs/stable/generated/torch.full.html) specifies that fill_value should be a scalar value. The documentation for nn.Parameter describes it as a Tensor subclass. Passing an nn.Parameter where a scalar is expected should raise a TypeError in eager mode, which is the correct behavior per the documented API.

The torch.compile troubleshooting guide discusses correctness issues where compiled code may diverge from eager mode. This is a general class of problems where torch.compile may be more permissive than eager mode because Dynamo's tracing bypasses Python-level type checks.

This appears to be a case where torch.compile's tracing silently accepts an input that violates the documented API. The recommended approach is to always validate model correctness in eager mode first. If torch.compile silently accepts code that eager mode rejects, that is a correctness gap in the compiler. The documentation does not offer a workaround beyond ensuring your code is valid in eager mode before compiling.

The torch.compile troubleshooting guide (https://pytorch.org/docs/stable/torch.compiler_troubleshooting.html) explains that graph breaks occur when TorchDynamo encounters Python constructs it cannot trace. The getting started guide notes that fullgraph=True will raise an error rather than silently inserting a graph break.

The official documentation does not provide a specific list of supported itertools functions. Dynamo operates by tracing Python bytecode, and when it encounters __next__ on an itertools.count object, it cannot symbolically trace this because itertools.count is a C-implemented iterator with mutable state. This falls into the documented category of unsupported Python constructs.

The recommended approach is to use torch._dynamo.explain() to identify graph breaks, then refactor the code to avoid the unsupported construct. For itertools.count specifically, replace it with a plain Python variable or a tensor-based counter that Dynamo can trace. If fullgraph=True is required, all such unsupported Python iterators must be removed from the compiled region.

The official PyTorch documentation does not cover internal implementation details of TorchDynamo's NNModuleVariable or the wrap_values() method, as these are internal compiler components not part of the public API. The torch.compile API reference documents the user-facing interface, and the Dynamo overview provides a high-level description of how Dynamo traces Python bytecode.

The torch.compile troubleshooting guide recommends using TORCHDYNAMO_REPRO_AFTER to generate a minimal reproducer when an internal error occurs. The NameError referencing a free variable 'fn' in wrap_values() is an internal compiler regression, not a user-facing API issue.

Since this is a bug introduced in a specific PR affecting Dynamo internals, the official documentation does not and would not cover this. The only relevant guidance is from the troubleshooting page: pin to a known-good nightly build if you encounter regressions, and file a GitHub issue with the minimal reproducer.

The PyTorch distributed documentation (https://pytorch.org/docs/stable/distributed.html) describes the torch.distributed package but does not include documentation for dist.record_comm as part of the stable public API.

The torch.compile troubleshooting guide explains that graph breaks occur when Dynamo encounters functions it cannot trace, including pybind11-bound C++ functions. When dist.record_comm internally calls torch._C._distributed_c10d functions, Dynamo cannot trace through them because they cross the Python-C++ boundary.

The general pattern for handling untraceable operations is to either: (1) allow graph breaks (the default), (2) use torch.compiler.allow_in_graph() to manually mark functions as safe, or (3) wrap the untraceable operation in a custom operator using torch.library. However, none of these are specifically documented for dist.record_comm, and adding proper Dynamo support would require changes to the PyTorch compiler internals.

The official PyTorch documentation discusses the Inductor CPU backend in the tutorial on debugging and profiling (https://docs.pytorch.org/tutorials/intermediate/inductor_debug_cpu.html). This covers how TorchInductor generates C++/Triton kernels for CPU execution. For CPU targets, Inductor typically generates C++ kernels using OpenMP parallelism rather than Triton, though experimental CPU Triton support exists.

However, the official pytorch.org documentation does not specifically address the CPU Triton backend's handling of combo kernels or argument size limitations. The CPU Triton backend is an experimental feature whose configuration details are not covered in the public documentation.

If you encounter failures with the CPU Triton backend and combo kernels, you may consider falling back to the default C++ CPU backend by not specifying the Triton CPU backend explicitly. The torch.compile troubleshooting guide offers general strategies for diagnosing compilation failures, such as using TORCH_LOGS to inspect generated code.

The PyTorch documentation on numerical accuracy (https://docs.pytorch.org/docs/stable/notes/numerical_accuracy.html) explains that numerical differences between eager and compiled modes are expected in certain scenarios due to floating-point non-associativity, operator fusion, and reordering of accumulations.

The FractionalMaxPool2d operator is documented at https://docs.pytorch.org/docs/stable/generated/torch.nn.FractionalMaxPool2d.html. The stochastic nature of the pooling region selection means that intermediate computation ordering can affect which maximum values are selected, particularly at region boundaries.

When 'significant' output differences occur beyond normal floating-point tolerance, this typically indicates a bug in the compiled kernel rather than expected numerical noise. The troubleshooting guide recommends comparing outputs systematically between eager and compiled modes to isolate whether the issue is in graph capture or code generation.

The official AOTInductor documentation (https://docs.pytorch.org/docs/stable/user_guide/torch_compiler/torch.compiler_aot_inductor.html) describes the ahead-of-time compilation workflow: torch.export() to capture the graph, then torch._inductor.aot_compile() to produce a shared library that can be loaded in C++ without Python.

However, the documentation does not discuss CUDA stream support within AOTInductor-compiled artifacts. The current documentation focuses on single-stream execution semantics and does not describe APIs for multi-stream execution or stream-aware kernel scheduling.

This represents a genuine gap in the documentation. If your use case requires CUDA stream support in AOTInductor-compiled code, the official docs do not currently provide guidance. You would need to manage streams externally at the C++ runtime level when invoking the compiled artifact, though no official documentation describes this pattern.

The official PyTorch documentation provides a dedicated page for this exact category of problem: Troubleshooting GuardOnDataDependentSymNode Errors (https://docs.pytorch.org/docs/stable/user_guide/torch_compiler/compile/dynamic_shapes_troubleshooting_guardon_errors.html). This page explains that when torch.compile encounters operations whose output depends on runtime tensor values, it raises a GuardOnDataDependentSymNode error. Printing a boolean derived from tensor data is a classic example.

The Dynamic Shapes Core Concepts page explains the underlying mechanism: torch.compile uses symbolic shapes, and data-dependent guards tie the compiled graph to specific tensor values, effectively defeating compilation. When you call print() on a boolean that depends on tensor data, Python's eager evaluation forces the symbolic value to be resolved.

The troubleshooting guide suggests: (1) use torch.compiler.is_compiling() to guard print statements so they only execute outside compilation, (2) avoid materializing tensor-data-dependent booleans in control flow, or (3) use TORCH_LOGS='guards' to diagnose which guard triggered the failure. Wrapping the print in `if not torch.compiler.is_compiling():` is the recommended fix.

The official PyTorch documentation does not provide detailed coverage of the torch._inductor.config.skip_l1_cache configuration option or its interaction with tensor memory layouts. The Inductor configuration namespace is largely an internal API without a comprehensive reference on pytorch.org.

The TorchInductor GPU Profiling tutorial discusses some Inductor configuration options in the context of performance profiling but does not cover cache-level optimization flags. Tensor layout affects memory access patterns, and cache optimization hints like skip_l1_cache would naturally interact with how data is laid out in memory.

Since the official documentation does not cover this specific config option, the recommended troubleshooting approach is to use TORCH_LOGS='output_code' to inspect how the flag affects generated kernel code. If skip_l1_cache is causing failures with certain tensor layouts, ensuring tensors are contiguous via .contiguous() before compilation, or disabling the flag, would be pragmatic workarounds.

The official PyTorch documentation covers both the dynamic and fullgraph parameters of torch.compile. The API reference defines dynamic as controlling whether tensor shapes are traced symbolically and fullgraph as requiring the entire function be captured in a single graph.

The Dynamic Shapes documentation and the Troubleshooting Dynamic Shapes page describe common failure modes when dynamic shapes are enabled. When combined with fullgraph=True, any operation that would normally cause a graph break instead raises an error. Dynamic shapes can trigger additional graph breaks that would not occur with static shapes.

The recommended diagnostic approach: first try compiling with fullgraph=False to see if the model compiles with graph breaks, then use TORCH_LOGS='graph_breaks' to identify what is causing them. If specific operations are incompatible with dynamic shapes, you can mark certain dimensions as static using torch._dynamo.mark_static() or use the dim-based dynamic_shapes API to selectively control which dimensions are dynamic.

When using custom autograd.Function subclasses with torch.compile, dtype mismatches can arise because the Dynamo tracer performs type inference on the captured graph. The official documentation on extending autograd (https://docs.pytorch.org/docs/stable/notes/extending.html) describes how to properly implement autograd.Function with setup_context and static forward/backward methods, which is the recommended pattern for torch.compile compatibility.

The Compiled Autograd tutorial (https://docs.pytorch.org/tutorials/intermediate/compiled_autograd_tutorial.html) explains that torch.compile captures the backward graph and optimizes it through Inductor. If your custom autograd function performs operations that change dtypes implicitly, the compiler's type inference may fail. You should ensure that all tensor operations within your custom forward and backward methods use consistent dtypes, and add explicit .to(dtype) calls where necessary.

Since this issue is resolved, the fix likely involved ensuring dtype consistency within the custom autograd function or updating to a version with improved type inference. Consider rewriting your custom function using torch.library.custom_op which provides better compiler integration than raw autograd.Function.

The share_memory_() failure with FakeTensor during torch.compile graph capture is related to how Dynamo traces operations using FakeTensors as proxies. The FakeTensor documentation (https://docs.pytorch.org/docs/stable/user_guide/torch_compiler/torch.compiler_fake_tensor.html) explains that FakeTensors carry metadata but no actual data. Operations that mutate storage-level properties — such as share_memory_(), which moves storage into shared memory for multiprocessing — are fundamentally incompatible with FakeTensor tracing.

When Dynamo encounters share_memory_(), it attempts to execute it on a FakeTensor which has no real underlying storage, resulting in an error. The recommended workaround is to move the share_memory_() call outside the compiled region. Call share_memory_() on your tensors before they enter the torch.compile-decorated function, or wrap the call in a torch._dynamo.disable block.

Since this issue is resolved, the fix may also have involved a PyTorch-side improvement to handle or skip share_memory_() during FakeTensor tracing.

The view_as_complex operation (https://docs.pytorch.org/docs/main/generated/torch.view_as_complex.html) reinterprets a real-valued tensor with a last dimension of size 2 as a complex-valued tensor. When used inside a fused Multi-Head Attention implementation under torch.compile with the Inductor backend, this can trigger compilation failures because Inductor's code generation has limited support for complex tensor operations.

The Inductor backend generates optimized Triton or C++ kernels, and complex number support in these generated kernels is incomplete. When view_as_complex is fused into a larger attention kernel, the Inductor codegen may fail to produce valid code. The official documentation does not explicitly enumerate which operations are unsupported in Inductor for complex tensors.

Since this issue is unresolved, practical workarounds include: (1) avoiding view_as_complex inside the compiled function and manually handling real and imaginary components as separate real-valued tensors, (2) using graph breaks to isolate the complex view operation, or (3) applying rotary embeddings using real-valued arithmetic rather than complex multiplication.

TorchDynamo traces Python bytecode and installs guards on values it observes to determine when a cached compiled graph can be reused. The TorchDynamo Deep Dive documentation (https://docs.pytorch.org/docs/2.2/torch.compiler_deepdive.html) explains the guard system. However, function.__defaults__ (the tuple of default argument values) was not being guarded, meaning that if a function's default arguments changed between calls, Dynamo would silently reuse the previously compiled graph with stale default values, producing incorrect results.

This is a silent correctness issue — the model simply produces wrong outputs with no error or warning. The Dynamic Shapes Core Concepts documentation discusses how guards and specializations interact but does not specifically mention __defaults__ as a guarded attribute.

Since this issue is resolved, the fix was to add a guard on function.__defaults__ so that Dynamo detects when default argument values change and triggers recompilation. If you encounter similar silent correctness issues, compare eager-mode and compiled-mode outputs and use torch._dynamo.config.verbose = True to inspect installed guards.

Regional compilation in Inductor allows compiling specific regions of a model rather than the entire graph. Higher-Order Primitives (HOPs) take other functions or subgraphs as arguments. The official PyTorch documentation does not provide a dedicated page covering this interaction.

Since regional compilation processes subgraphs independently, HOPs pose a challenge because they inherently span multiple subgraphs. The Inductor backend needs to handle the inner callable's compilation in the context of the outer HOP, which the regional path did not support.

Since this issue is resolved, the fix involved adding HOP support to the regional Inductor code path. The documentation has a gap: there is no pytorch.org page documenting which features are supported by regional vs. full-graph compilation. If you encounter this error on an older PyTorch version, avoid using regional compilation when your model contains HOPs, and instead use full-graph torch.compile. Upgrading to a version with the fix is recommended.

The torch.autograd.Function.jvp static method is documented in the Extending PyTorch guide (https://docs.pytorch.org/docs/stable/notes/extending.html). The jvp method enables forward-mode automatic differentiation for custom autograd functions. However, having a jvp method defined but not actually used can cause confusing shape errors during torch.compile tracing.

The issue arises because torch.compile inspects the autograd.Function subclass to determine which differentiation paths to trace. When a jvp method is present, the compiler may attempt to set up forward-mode AD tracing infrastructure including tangent tensors. If the jvp method's implementation does not align with actual tensor shapes, the result is an inscrutable shape error.

Since this issue is unresolved, the practical workaround is to remove the unused jvp method from your autograd.Function subclass if you do not need forward-mode AD. Only define jvp if you actively use torch.func.jvp or torch.autograd.functional.jvp. The official docs do not currently warn that an unused jvp can trigger compilation errors, which represents a documentation gap.
